segments.s198-g1: ازان پس is an archaic/poetic sequence meaning 'after that' (از + آن + پس). خبر شد here is an impersonal or intransitive verbal phrase 'it became known / news came' (شد used as full past verb). بافراسیاب is a prepositional/compound form using با + (name) to indicate the person to whom the news came—'to/for Afrasiab'.
segments.s198-g2: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. شد is the past tense 'became'. مرز توران is the noun phrase 'the border/frontier of Turan' (noun + proper name in genitive relationship). چو is the simile 'like/as', followed by دریای آب 'a sea of water' (دریای is the construct form of دریا + izafet and آب is its descriptor/literal object).
segments.s198-g3: سوی + place means 'toward / to' that place; کاسه‌رود is the place-name (compound) and اندر آمد is a poetic order where اندر (in/into) precedes آمد 'came' — together 'came into'. سپاه is the subject 'the army/host' which follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s198-g4: زمین شد is 'the land became' (شد = became). ز before a noun here gives cause/reason 'because of' (poetic preposition). کین سیاوش is a genitive phrase 'Siavash's vengeance' (کین = vengeance, followed by the name), and سیاه is a predicative adjective 'black / dark' describing the land.
segments.s198-g5: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed / military commander' (subject). به + noun marks the indirect object 'to' (here 'به پیران سالار' = 'to Piran-salar'); پیران سالار is a compound vocative/title (Piran as elder/leader + سالار 'chief/commander'). گفت is the past tense 'said' (here a reporting verb); note the poetic diction can place the indirect object before the verb.
segments.s198-g6: که introduces the content of what was said ('that ...'). خسرو functions as the actor 'the king' (can be a name or the title 'king'); سخن is 'speech/words' and برگشاد is a past/adjectival verbal form 'opened/revealed' (بر- + گشاد). از نهفت is an ablative phrase 'out of/from concealment' (از = from, نهفت = hidden), describing how the speech was revealed.
segments.s198-g7: مگر at the start of a clause functions here as a conditional 'unless / if not'. کین سخن is 'this speech of vengeance' (کین = vengeance modifying سخن). را is the definite object marker attached to the noun phrase. پذیره شویم is a periphrastic expression: پذیره (reception/acceptance) plus شویم (1st plural present/subjunctive of شدن) yields 'we accept / let us accept' or in negative conditional 'unless we accept'.
segments.s198-g8: همه 'all / everyone' is the subject of the implied exhortation. با + instruments (درفش و تبیره) means 'with the banner and the tabireh' (a paired instrumental phrase); و is the coordinating 'and'. شویم again is 1st plural subjunctive/optative of شدن used here as a hortative 'let us become / let us be' — i.e. 'let us all (be) with banner and tabireh'.
segments.s198-g129: Word order is inverted in poetic narrative: بدو (to him) is an indirect-object prepositional/adverbial element placed before the verb گفت (said). بهرام is the speaker and appears after the verb (common in poetry). با من is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase meaning “with me,” and تژاو follows as the noun in that phrase (here treated as a noun/name or noun phrase affiliated with ‘with me’).
segments.s198-g130: چو is the comparative/simile marker “as/like.” The preposition با introduces the opponent (با شیر درنده = “with a ferocious lion”), where شیر is ‘lion’ and درنده is an adjectival modifier ‘ferocious/predatory.’ پیکار is the noun ‘battle/struggle’ and گاو (here) is the opposing animal ‘cow/bull’; the order (verb omitted) is characteristic of epic simile: ‘like a bull contending with a fierce lion.’
segments.s198-g131: سرش is سر + enclitic ش ‘his’ (the clitic immediately follows the noun). را is the direct-object marker signaling that ‘his head’ is the object. بخنجر is an instrumental phrase ‘with a dagger.’ ببرید here functions as the past action ‘cut off’ (poetic past of بریدن). پست is an adjective ‘base/despicable’ commenting on the agent (or used as an epithet for the offender).
segments.s198-g132: بفتراک is an adverbial/phrase meaning something like ‘apart/away’ or ‘separately’ (here indicating the head detached). زین is ‘saddle’ and کیانی is an adjective/possessive attributing the saddle to the Kayanian dynasty (‘Kayanian/royal’). ببست is the past tense ‘he bound/fastened’ (بستن). Together: ‘he fastened it to the Kayanian saddle.’
segments.s198-g133: بلشکر is an adverbial/phrase ‘with an army’ or ‘bringing an army’ used to set the scene. گه here is an adverb (occasionally/then) accompanying the verb آورد (past ‘brought’ or ‘caused to come’). و links the clauses. بفگند is a past verb from افکندن with the prefix be- meaning ‘cast/scattered’ (he scattered/put to flight). خوار is a predicative adjective ‘despised/humbled,’ describing the result inflicted on the foe.
segments.s198-g134: نه ... نه ... is the correlative ‘neither ... nor ...’ linking two noun phrases. نام‌آوری is ‘renown/fame,’ modified by بد (‘bad/ill’ here producing the notion of ill-repute or simply qualifying the kind of fame), and گردی سوار functions as a compound nominal phrase meaning a group/host of riders (گردی modifying سوار). Grammatically this line denies the presence of either renown or mounted troops.
segments.s198-g121: This clause is a two-part relative/question construction. The initial که functions as an interrogative/relative 'who/that'; ایدر 'here' follows and locates the action; فرستنده 'sender' is the noun, and تو 'you' is the person sent. The second که introduces the subordinate relative clause 'who was' with بود the past copula 'was' (archaic word order places the location and agent before the relative verb).
segments.s198-g122: کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who/whom'; خواستی is the past/preterite second-person form 'you wanted/sought'. زین here is a fused/poetic prepositional phrase ('ز این' = 'from these') but the provided lemma senses do not include this use, so we mark it separately; بزرگان is the plural 'great ones/nobles'; بسود is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to the advantage of/for the benefit of'. The clause asks whom he sought among the nobles for advantage.
segments.s198-g123: ببهرام is the prepositional/agent marker + name meaning 'by/for Bahram' (here introducing the speaker). گفت is the verb 'said' (the lemma senses list is not the reporting-verb sense, so we map it as absent). ار is the conditional 'if'. دهی is the present/subjunctive/second-person form 'you give' (here conditional). زینهار is a noun 'warning/assurance' (poetic use meaning a pledge or assurance).
segments.s198-g124: بگویم is the first-person singular subjunctive/future-like 'I will say / I will tell'; the clitic ت is the 2nd-person object suffix ('you') attached separately here; را is the definite-object marker emphasizing the direct object; هرچ means 'whatever' (introducing a free relative); پرسی is the present/subjunctive/second-person 'you ask' (here 'you may ask'); ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from'; کار is 'matter/affair'. Together: 'I will tell you whatever you ask about the matter.'
segments.s198-g125: تژاوست is an archaic/poetic form combining a name (Tžāv/Tžāv-) with the old copula -ست (='is'); here it states the identity of the person. شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. فرستنده‌ام is a noun + copula clitic meaning 'I am (a) messenger/envoy' (فرستنده 'messenger' used predicatively with -ام 'I am').
segments.s198-g126: بنزدیک is a locative/adverb 'near, close at hand'. او 'he' is the third-person pronoun. من is the emphatic subject 'I'. پرستنده‌ام is a participle/noun 'worshipper/devotee' used predicatively with the first-person copula -ام 'I am (a) devotee/adorer'. The clause literally places the speaker close to that person and identifies himself as his devotee.
segments.s198-g127: مکش is the negative imperative of کشیدن 'do not pull/strike/kill' (here 'do not kill/slay'). مر and مرا are two object pronouns for 'me' — a poetic/emphatic duplication (مر is an older form, مرا the regular object form). تا is a subordinating conjunction 'so that / until'. نمایمت is the first-person singular verb 'I will show' with the second-person object suffix -ت 'to you' (نمای- + مت = 'I show you' / 'let me show you'). راه is the direct object 'road/way'.
segments.s198-g128: بجایی here functions as 'to the place' (poetic form of 'به جایی'); که is the relative pronoun 'where/that'; او 'he' is the subject; دارد is the present/past-auxiliary 'has/possesses' (here 'has' = 'is buried at'); آرامگاه is 'tomb/resting-place'. The relative clause gives the destination: 'to the place where he has his tomb.'
segments.s198-g73: ازین رفتن من: a topical prepositional phrase made of از ('of/about') + ین (orthographic 'این' = 'this') + رفتنِ من ('my going'). ندارد ایچ غم is literal 'does not have any sorrow' where ندار is the negative present/predicate 'has not' and ایچ (poetic) = 'anything/at all'. Word order places the 'about this going of mine' phrase before the predicate 'I have no sorrow'.
segments.s198-g74: که introduces a subordinate/casual clause ('for/because/that'), من is the explicit subject, کوه خارا is the object phrase 'the flinty/rock mountain' (خارا here = flint/rock), and بسوزم is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'I might burn / I could burn' expressing potential; به دم is an instrumental phrase 'with a breath' (به used adverbially). Together it gives the reason: 'for I could burn the flinty mountain with a breath.'
segments.s198-g75: بسختی is an adverbial fronted adverb meaning 'with difficulty.' گذشت is the past tense verb 'it passed.' از در کاسه‌رود is a prepositional phrase 'through/from the gate/entrance of the basin-stream' (در here = 'gate/entrance'). The clause describes a difficult passage.
segments.s198-g76: جهان را is topicalized 'the world' marked by را; همه رنج ('all suffering') is the theme and برف آب is a compound-like expression 'snow-water' (meltwater). بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause states that the world's entire suffering consisted of snow-melt water.
segments.s198-g77: چو آمد is a temporal connector 'when it came.' بران کوه = 'to/on that mountain' (بران = 'upon/thereon'), هیزم فراز places the object 'firewood' with the locative/goal 'fraz' ('fraz' = summit/top), so literally 'firewood came up onto that mountain's summit.' The word order is compact and somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s198-g78: ندانست is the past negative 'he did not know' (could not judge). بالا و پهناش coordinates two dimensions 'height and its breadth' (پهناش = 'its breadth/expanse'), and باز here is an adjectival/poetic qualifier 'open/extended' or 'wide' — together conveying inability to reckon the mountain's height or extent.
segments.s198-g79: ز here marks source/agent 'from.' پیکان تیر is a pleonastic poetic doublet 'arrows/shafts,' آتشی is an attributive adjective 'fiery,' and برفروخت is a past verbal form 'kindled/ignited' (causal or intransitive 'a blaze was kindled'). The phrase says that fiery arrows set a fire.
segments.s198-g80: بکوه اندر افگند is a verbal clause '(he) cast (it) into the mountain' where بکوه = 'into/to the mountain,' اندر = 'in/within,' and افگند is the past 'threw/cast.' و links the clauses and هیزم بسوخت is a resultant passive/intransitive past 'the firewood burned.' The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s198-g25: تبه شد is a past verbal phrase meaning 'were ruined/was ruined': تبه here denotes ruin and شد is the past of شدن 'become/occur'. بسی مردم و چارپای is the affected group (many people and beasts) placed after the predicate in poetic word order.
segments.s198-g26: یکی را نبد is an archaic negative-existential construction: را marks 'یکی' and نبد ('was not') states that there was not a single one. چنگ و بازو بجای is the noun phrase 'claw and arm in place'—بجای means 'in place'—so the whole means 'there was not one who had claw or arm in its proper place.'
segments.s198-g27: بهشتم is بهشت 'paradise/heaven' with the possessive -م 'my' (poetic locative/possessive). برآمد is the past 'rose/appeared', and بلند آفتاب is the noun phrase 'the high/tall sun' describing what rose; the order is poetically inverted (locative → verb → adjective + noun).
segments.s198-g28: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد = became). سراسر 'entire' modifies the result, and چو introduces a simile ('like/as'): چو دریای آب = 'like a sea of water.' This is a standard verbless-result clause framed by شدن + simile.
segments.s198-g29: سپهبد is the subject 'spahbed/commander.' سپه را uses را to mark the definite object 'the army.' همی گرد کرد = 'made (them) turn/encircle' where همی is an archaic/poetic adverb often accompanying past narration and گرد کرد (lit. 'did around') = 'turned/caused to turn.'
segments.s198-g30: سخن رفت is an idiom: رفت ('went') with سخن yields 'speech went' → 'talk was had / talk continued.' چندی is temporal 'for a while.' ز روز نبرد is a poetic genitive/oblique 'about/of the day of battle' (ز = 'of/about').
segments.s198-g31: که introduces the causal clause ('because/for'), ایدر = 'here' (locative), and سپه شد ز تنگی تباه = 'the army became ruined from/through scarcity' — شد is past 'became', ز expresses cause ('because of/from'), and تباه is the resulting state 'ruined.'
segments.s198-g32: سزدگر is an impersonal/fixed form meaning 'if it seems fitting' or 'it is fitting (that)...' برانیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of راندن 'to drive/lead' meaning here 'let us withdraw/drive away'; ازین = از + این 'from this', and رزمگاه = 'battlefield.' The clause expresses a tentative proposal.
segments.s198-g17: This is a nominal phrase followed by a predicative noun with the past copula: 'سراپرده و خیمه‌ها' (the inner chambers and the tents) is the subject, then 'گشت یخ' = 'became ice/froze.' Here گشت is the past of گشتن used in the sense 'became/turned' and یخ is the predicate (turned to ice). The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s198-g18: کشید is the past of کشیدن used intransitively here meaning 'stretched/ran (a line)'; the phrase 'از بر کوه بر برف' is a sequence of prepositions: از (from) + بر کوه (from/on the mountain) + بر برف (onto/upon the snow) — a poetic use of از + بر to indicate origin and destination. نخ ('thread/line') is the concrete thing that 'stretched' and appears after the verb (verb before object) as is common in poetry.
segments.s198-g19: بیک هفته is a poetic duration expression (بیِ یک 'for one') meaning 'for a week.' کس here is negative 'no one' (contextual negation with ندید). 'روی هامون' = 'the surface/face of the plain' (روی = surface); ندید is the past negative 'did not see.' Word order: duration phrase → (no) person → object → verb.
segments.s198-g20: This clause states the consequence: 'همه کشور' (the whole country/realm) is the subject, 'از برف' ('because of/from the snow') gives the cause, and 'شد ناپدید' is a compound predicate meaning 'became vanished' (i.e. was made invisible/vanished). شد is the past copula 'became' and ناپدید is the resulting adjective 'vanished.'
segments.s198-g21: A nominal list 'خور و خواب و آرامگه' (food, sleep, and resting-place) serves as the subject; 'تنگ شد' is a fixed expression meaning 'became scarce/tight/limited.' شد is the past copula. The conjunctions و simply join the nouns in the list.
segments.s198-g22: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said' (تو = you; گفتی = you said in the past) introducing reported speech with که ('that'). Inside the reported clause 'روی زمین سنگ شد' the subject 'روی زمین' (the surface/face of the earth) precedes the predicate 'سنگ شد' ('became stone' i.e. froze/hardened).
segments.s198-g23: This is an existential/negative construction: 'کسی را نبد یاد روز نبرد' literally 'there was no one (کسی را نبد) who had the memory of the day of battle.' را is the object/topic marker used in the idiom; نبد (نبود) expresses non-existence/absence ('there was not'). یاد روز نبرد = 'memory/remembrance of the day of battle.'
segments.s198-g24: همی is an archaic/progressive particle here meaning 'continually' or describing repeated/action in progress. 'اسپ جنگی' = 'war-horse' (اسپ = horse; جنگی = warlike/for war). بکشت و بخورد are classical past forms meaning 'killed and ate' (the prefixed ب- is an archaic verbal prefix seen in epic Persian). The two verbs describe the desperate action: they killed war-horses and ate them.
segments.s198-g41: هنوز sets a temporal/topicual frame 'still/yet' at the start. از بدی is a short prepositional phrase 'from/of evil' (از + noun). تا چه آیدت پیش is a classical sequence: تا 'until/ever', چه 'what', آیدت = آید + ت (3rd‑sg. present/subjunctive 'may come' with the enclitic 'to you'), and پیش 'forth/ahead'; together they mean 'what yet may come upon you'. The verb آید is present-subjunctive/optative in sense of 'may befall'.
segments.s198-g42: این زمان is a temporal phrase 'at this time' that locates the situation. The locative phrase به چرم اندر است uses به + noun + اندر (poetic 'in/within') + است 'is' to state location: 'is within the hide'. Note the subject گاومیش appears after the verb, an expected poetic inversion: 'this time the buffalo is in its hide.' اندر is the archaic/poetic equivalent of modern در.
segments.s198-g43: سپهبد is a title ('spahbed' / lieutenant‑general) serving as the subject/agent. چنین 'thus' modifies گفت 'said'. گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (classical past), introducing reported speech; the following name کاذرگشسپ is the content/person mentioned. (Note: the lemma senses provided for گفت did not list the verbal sense 'to say'.)
segments.s198-g44: نبد is an archaic negative/past copular sense ('was not' / 'did not exist' — here functioning like نبُود). نامورتر is the comparative 'more famed'. ز introduces the standard or domain of comparison/relation in poetic register ('in/for/from' → here 'in (the matter of) war'), and جنگی qualifies the domain as 'military/in war'. زرسپ is the standard of comparison (the name Zarsap). The clause reads 'was not more renowned in war than Zarsap.'
segments.s198-g45: بلشکر is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with an army / in force' (literally 'with army'). نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch / hold (it)', where نگه is a noun/command 'watch' and کن is the imperative of کردن. The conjunction که introduces the causal/subordinate clause, and چون here functions as 'when/as' leading into the example/name ریونیز.
segments.s198-g46: This is the subordinate content clause introduced by که: بینی is 2nd person singular present/subjunctive 'you see' (used here with future/optative force 'you will see'). بمردی is adverbial 'in manhood/manfully' modifying how he is seen. و دیدار نیز adds 'and (his) sight/appearance also' — دیدار here functions as 'sight/appearance/meeting'. The whole yields 'that when you see him you will behold his manliness and his appearance as well.'
segments.s198-g47: نه is the negation 'not/no'. بر بی‌گنه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the innocent' (بر + noun). کشته is a past participle 'slain/killed' used adjectivally, آمد is the past tense verb 'came', and فرود (here adverbial/noun) 'down' completes the verbal idea 'came down' (فرود آمد). Word order is the older poetic order 'نه بر بی‌گنه کشته آمد فرود' = 'he did not come down slain upon the innocent' → 'he was not slain upon innocents.'
segments.s198-g48: نوشته چنین بود is a fixed formula 'it is written thus' or 'the writing said thus' (نوشته = 'written/it is written'). The repeated بود بود آنچ بود is rhetorical/past-tense emphasis: multiple copies of the copula 'was' surrounding آنچ ('that which/what') to assert 'thus it was — that which was.' This is an emphatic, somewhat formulaic epic clause signalling 'so it was written / so it was.'
segments.s198-g9: وگرنه is a conditional/adversative 'otherwise'. ز ایران is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = from). بیاید is a third-person present-subjunctive/optative ('may/should come' — used here for a hypothetical/conditional event). سپاه is the subject 'host/army'; the verb precedes the noun (inverted order for poetic effect): 'if from Iran a host should come'.
segments.s198-g10: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. بینیم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/optative ('we may/would see'), here used for the hypothetical result. روشن is a predicate adjective modifying خورشید ('bright/illuminated'). The phrase is elliptical: 'neither (the) sun (we) see bright, nor (the) moon'.
segments.s198-g11: This opens with two imperatives: برو ('go') and آور ('bring'), forming a command 'go, bring (the) army'. لشکر is the direct object 'army/host'. ز هر سو فراز is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from every side, upward/on high' (فراز 'on high/above'). Word order is compressed for force: two short commands followed by the direction.
segments.s198-g12: سخنها 'the words/speeches' is the (impersonal) subject. نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'. The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause; گردد is the subjunctive/passive-ish form 'become' or 'happen to be', and دراز is the predicate adjective 'long'. Together: 'The speeches must not become long' (i.e. do not let talk be protracted).
segments.s198-g13: وزین رو is a poetic locution ('that/this face/side' or 'from that quarter') used adverbially; برآمد is simple past 'arose/issued forth'. یکی تندباد is an indefinite/partitive phrase 'a certain/one fierce gale' (یکی = 'one/a certain', تندباد = 'gale'). The clause states the emergence of the storm.
segments.s198-g14: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/so that it was the case that'. کس را uses را as an object/experiencer marker ('to anyone' / 'for anyone'); ز ایران 'from Iran' (here referring to the people of Iran). نبد is an archaic negative/past sense 'did not have / was lacking' (i.e. there was no such thing for them). رزم یاد is a noun phrase 'memory/remembrance of battle'. The whole: 'so that no one from Iran retained remembrance of war.'
segments.s198-g15: یکی ابر تند اندر آمد is a simple narrative: یکی 'a certain/one', ابر 'cloud', تند 'swift/violent', اندر آمد 'came in/entered'. چو introduces a temporal/comparative clause 'when/as', and گرد here is used as a noun 'dust/whirl' (i.e. as the cloud whipped up like dust). The clause prepares the following result clause.
segments.s198-g16: ز سرما 'from the cold' gives the cause/origin. همی is an archaic/progressive particle often marking repeated or emphatic action ('then/just/indeed' here giving immediacy). لب بدندان is an idiomatic pairing 'lips against the teeth' (describing the effect of the cold). فسرد is the verb meaning 'became cold / froze' (3rd person past) — note that the lemma/senses supplied in the word-list do not match this archaic/weather-verb use, so no listed sense corresponds exactly.
segments.s198-g113: کمان را is a noun phrase with the definite/object marker را after the noun (object-first order common in classical Persian). بزه کرد is a past verbal phrase where کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made' attached to بزه (an older or poetic verbal/noun element here); و coordinates to a second verb. بفشارد is an archaic/poetic past-form (classical spelling with -د) meaning 'he pressed/squeezed' (from فشرد) and ران is its object 'thigh/flank' (no را, which is optional).
segments.s198-g114: درآمد is a past verb 'came forth/rose/sprang' placed before its source phrase (verb-first ordering often used in poetry). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', جای 'place', آن the demonstrative 'that', هیون a poetic noun 'beast/animal', and گران an adjective 'heavy/weighty' modifying هیون. Together they mean 'that heavy beast sprang from its place.'
segments.s198-g115: یکی تیر is a simple quantifier + noun 'one arrow'. بگشاد is a classical/perfective form 'opened' (ب‑ prefix in older Persian verbs is common in poetry). نگشاد is the same root with the negative prefix ن‑, 'did not open' (here the pair بگشاد و نگشاد creates a contrast/condition). لب 'lip' is the affected noun; the clause thus links 'one arrow' and the (opened/not-opened) state of the lip.
segments.s198-g116: کبوده (dark-blue) modifies the previous لب; نبود is the past negative 'was not'; ایچ (ایچ/هیچ) = 'anything/at all' here functions in the negative construction ایچ پیدا نبود 'nothing was visible'; پیدا 'visible' is predicate complement. ز شب = 'from the night' (poetic for 'in the night' / 'by night').
segments.s198-g117: بزد is the simple past 'he struck' (3rd singular). بر introduces the target 'upon/at' and governs the following noun phrase کمربند چوپان شاه; in classical Persian noun + modifier chain (کمربند followed by چوپان شاه) expresses a possessive/genitive relation ('the belt of the shepherd (of) the king' or 'the shepherd-king's belt') depending on context. The whole phrase is the prepositional object of بزد.
segments.s198-g118: همی is an aspectual/continuous particle often signaling ongoing or inchoative action in poetry ('began/still'); گشت is the past of 'become/turn'. رنگ is 'color/complexion' and is followed by two adjectival descriptors کبوده 'dark-blue' and سیاه 'black' (postpositive adjectives typical in Persian), so the clause means 'his color became dark-blue, black.'
segments.s198-g119: ز اسپ اندر افتاد is a prepositional phrase 'from the horse' (ز اسپ) plus the verb اندر افتاد 'fell down/was thrown (off)'. و links to the next clause. زنهار is an interjection/noun meaning an appeal like 'forbear/mercy/beware' and خواست is the past 'asked/sought' — together زنهار خواست means 'he cried for mercy' or 'he begged (saying "zanehār")'.
segments.s198-g120: بدو is the dative 'to him'. گفت is the past 'said'. The speaker's name بهرام is placed after گفت (a common poetic word order) identifying the speaker. برگوی is an imperative/command 'say/speak' (here in the reported speech) and راست is an adverb/adjective 'straight/true' — together forming the quoted command 'speak the truth' or 'tell it straight.'
segments.s198-g65: برنج و بسختی بپروردیم = a compound clause meaning “we raised (you) on rice and with difficulty.” مرا/تو is omitted here (object is understood). برنج is a direct noun (rice). بسختی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase formed with the prefix ب- meaning “with/through” + سختی “difficulty.” بپروردیم is the first-person plural past of پروردن (پروردیم) with the archaic/preverbal b- (common in epic Persian) — so literally “we reared/raised.” Word order: material/adverbials (برنج و بسختی) precede the verb, as typical in Persian.
segments.s198-g66: بگفتار هرگز نیازردیم = “by speech/words we never caused hurt.” بگفتار is ب- (by/in) + گفتار (speech, manner of speaking) and functions adverbially “in/with speech” or “by words.” هرگز is the adverb “never.” نیازردیم is first-person plural past negative of آزار دادن / آزردن (نیازردیم = we did not wound/offend). The negative is built into the verb form (نـ- + گذشته).
segments.s198-g67: مرا برد باید بدین کار دست = roughly “I must be brought/taken into this affair” (the clause is compact/poetic). مرا (here written as م + را in the gloss) is the object pronoun “me.” برد here functions as a verbal idea ‘to take/lead’ (poetic past infinitive/participle use) — the listed senses do not include the verbal sense, so the correct sense is not in the provided list. باید is the impersonal/modal “must/it is necessary.” بدین is ب- (with/in) + دین (here functioning as the demonstrative/part of بدین = “to/into this”), and کار دست = “the hand of the business” = “this work/affair” (دست following کار indicates involvement or the locus of action). Word order is compressed for poetic effect; the logical order: مرا باید برد به این کار = “I must be taken to/into this work.”
segments.s198-g68: نشاید تو با رنج و من با نشست = “It is not fitting that you (should be) with toil while I (am) with sitting.” نشاید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “it is not fitting/ought not to be.” The clause contrasts two circumstances introduced by با: تو با رنج “you with hardship/you endure toil” and من با نشست “and I with sitting” (نشست as a noun/gerund here = ‘sitting, remaining’). The coordinate conjunction و links the contrasted halves.
segments.s198-g69: بدو گفت گیو آنک من ساختم = “To him Giv said that I built (it).” بدو is the dative/enclitic ‘to him’ (بـ + او). گفت here is the verb ‘said’ (past of گفتن); the provided gloss entries list conversation/dialogue senses but not the past-verb form, so the exact verbal sense is not in the given list. گیو is the proper name (subject of the reported speech). آنک functions as the conjunction ‘that/for’ introducing reported content. من ساختم is the verb phrase “I built/made” (ساختم = first-person singular past of ساختن).
segments.s198-g70: بدین کار گردن برافراختم = “For/unto this task I raised my neck” (i.e. I put my neck forward/took responsibility). بدین is the prepositional/relative ب- + دین (= ‘this’) meaning “for/unto this.” کار is “task/affair.” گردن برافراختم is a fixed poetic expression: گردن (neck) + برافراختم (I raised/displayed/held up) — the verb برافراختن in past first-person (برافراختم) means “I lifted/raised,” used metaphorically for taking on duty or showing pride. Word order places the goal/affair (بدین کار) before the idiomatic verb phrase.
segments.s198-g71: کنون‌ای پسر گاه آرایشست = “Now, O son, (this) is a time of adornment.” کنون‌ای is the vocative temporal phrase: کنون “now” plus vocative ای addressing the youth. پسر here is vocative “O son/youth.” گاه is a noun “time/season” and آرایشست is the predicate ‘it is adornment/arrangement’ (آرایش + است fused in poetic form). The clause states that the present season is for display/preparation.
segments.s198-g72: نه هنگام پیری و بخشایشست = “not the time of old age and (not) of leniency/forgiveness.” نه negates the following noun phrase. هنگام پیری = “the time of old age” (هنگام = at the time of; پیری = old age). و بخشایشست adds a further predicate nominal “and (a time) of mercy/forgiveness” — بخشایشست is the fused form بخشایش + است meaning “it is mercy/forgiveness” or “a setting for mercy,” here negated by نه. Overall: this is not the season for aging and for leniency.
segments.s198-g89: چنانچون (‘as, just as’) introduces a clause of manner. ببایست is an archaic/impersonal past/modal meaning “it was fitting/necessary” or “one ought to,” and here it frames the action that follows. برساختند is the main verb in the simple past, 3rd person plural (“they built/formed/raised”), so the whole segment means “they formed/arranged as was fitting.”
segments.s198-g90: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase “from every direction/side” (ز = from, هر سو = every side). طلایه is the subject “the vanguard/forefront.” برون تاختند is the verb phrase: برون (‘forth/out’) + تاختند (past, 3rd pl. “they rushed/galloped/charged”), so the clause reads “the vanguard rushed forth from every side.”
segments.s198-g91: گروگرد is a noun meaning “throng/crowd.” بودی is the past form of بودن here (“there was/it was”). نشست is used as a noun “session/assembly” (rather than just the verb “to sit”), and تژاو qualifies it (“radiance/brightness”), so the phrase describes “there was a throng, a radiant assembly.”
segments.s198-g92: سواری is the noun “rider/horseman.” که introduces a relative clause “who/which.” بودیش is an older pronominal/verb form meaning roughly “it/they were” or “it belonged to him/it was of him” used here as the copula for the relative clause. با is the preposition “with,” شیر is “lion,” and تاو is an abstract noun “radiance/glow”; together the clause says the riders’ brilliance was with/like a lion’s radiance.
segments.s198-g93: فسیله is a noun meaning “interval/respite.” بدان here functions as a locative/composite (“in/at that”), parsed in the data as ب + دان; جایگه is “place,” and داشتی is 2nd person singular past “you had.” The sequence is a simple clause: “You had a respite at that place.”
segments.s198-g94: چنان is an adverb “thus/so.” کوه تا کوه is a poetic framing meaning “mountain to mountain” or “mountain against mountain” (تا here marks the juxtaposition/extent: ‘…to…’). بگذاشتی is 2nd person singular past of گذاشتن with the prefix ب- (here “you placed/put up” or “you made”), so the clause says you set/piled things so that mountain met mountain.
segments.s198-g95: خبر شد is an impersonal phrase “news happened/it became known.” که introduces the content clause “that …,” آمد is past “came,” ز ایران is the source “from Iran,” and سپاه is the subject “the army/host.” Together: “It became known that an army had come from Iran.”
segments.s198-g96: گله is “flock/herd.” برد here functions as the verb “to take/drive” (not matched by the supplied sense list for برد, so indexed -1). باید is the modal “must/ought,” به یکسو is the direction “to one side,” and ز راه = “off/from the road.” The clause therefore commands/notes that the herds must be moved to one side of the road.
segments.s198-g105: ازیدر is an uncertain locative/adverbial form (probably ‘from here’ or similar) — its exact reading is unclear. بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase where بر means “against” and takes the pronoun ایشان “them.” شبیخون is a compound noun “night-raid/surprise attack.” کنیم is the 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/hortative form of کردن used here as a hortative/imperative “let us do / let us carry out.”
segments.s198-g106: همه کوه is the object phrase “all the hills/mountains” (همه = “all,” کوه = “mountain”). در جنگ هامون is a locative/prepositional phrase “into / in the battle of Hamun” (در = “in”), with هامون naming the plain. کنیم is the same hortative-subjunctive “let us make / let us turn (them into).” Word order places the object phrase before the modifying prepositional phrase and the verb at the end, a common Persian/poetic order.
segments.s198-g107: کبوده بیامد: کبوده is an adjective/noun ‘dark-blue, dusky’ and بیامد is the simple past ‘came.’ چو = ‘like/as’ introduces the simile. گرد here is the noun ‘dust/whirl, i.e. a cloud or wreath of dust,’ and سیاه qualifies it ‘black.’ The clause reads literally ‘a dark-blue (body) came like a black dust-cloud.’
segments.s198-g108: شب تیره is a noun phrase ‘the dark night’ (شب + adjective تیره). نزدیک here functions as an intransitive/adverbial ‘approaching / drew near.’ ایران سپاه = ‘the Iranian host/army’ (the two nouns together name whose host). The clause means ‘the dark night approached near the Iranian army.’ Poetic word order places the adverb/verb-like نزدیک before the noun phrase it modifies.
segments.s198-g109: طلایه شب تیره is a genitival noun phrase ‘the vanguard/forefront of the dark night’ (طلایه takes the following noun phrase as its complement). بود is the past copula ‘was,’ and بهرام is the predicate nominative — typical poetic inversion: ‘At the vanguard of the dark night was Bahram.’
segments.s198-g110: کمندش = کمند + -ش (possessive suffix) ‘his lasso/snare.’ سر پیل را is a definite object phrase ‘the elephant’s head’ (سر = head; را marks the definite object). دام بود = ‘was a trap/snare’ (دام = trap; بود = was). The whole clause states ‘his lasso was a trap for the elephant’s head.’
segments.s198-g111: برآورد here is a past verb ‘he brought forth/caused’ (in older/poetic Persian it can mean ‘produced’ or ‘raised’). اسپ کبوده is the noun phrase ‘the dark-coloured steed’ (اسپ = steed/horse; کبوده = dark). خروش is the direct object or resultant ‘a cry/roar.’ The clause reads ‘he caused the dark steed to give a cry’ or ‘the dark steed raised a cry.’
segments.s198-g112: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase ‘from the host/army’ (ز = from). برافراخت is a past tense verb ‘he raised/hoisted/uttered.’ بهرام is the subject name ‘Bahram.’ گوش here is used in the sense of ‘attention / what reaches the ear’ (poetic use: raising a sound/cry). Literally: ‘From the army Bahram raised (a) cry/attention.’
segments.s198-g49: The clause begins with an existential/old-fashioned particle 'م' (fused here with the following clitic) introducing 'مرا جام' (lit. 'to me a cup'). 'را' appears as the enclitic marking/relativizing the preceding element. 'جام' is the noun 'cup', 'ازو' ('from him') marks source, and 'پر' is a predicate adjective meaning 'full (of)'. The sequence 'می‌و شیر' names two things filling the cup (an archaic/poetic pairing: 'mi'/'wine' written here as 'می‌و' and 'شیر' 'milk'), followed by the past copula 'بود' ('was'). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: existential + indirect object + noun + genitive/source + predicate + copula.
segments.s198-g50: 'جوان را' is fronted and topicalized (the 'را' here functions as a topic/emphasis marker), 'ز بالا' is a prepositional phrase 'from above'. 'سخن' is the subject 'word/speech' and 'تیر بود' is a predicative metaphor: 'was an arrow' (تیر used metaphorically). 'بود' is the past copula. The clause means that a word coming from above struck the young man like an arrow; the order is poetic (topic — source — subject — predicate).
segments.s198-g51: 'کنون' is the temporal adverb 'now'. 'از گذشته' literally 'from the past' indicates the domain/source of what follows. 'نیاریم' is an archaic negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'we do not bring/recall' (i.e. 'we do not remember/recall'), and 'یاد' is the object 'remembrance/memory'. Together they mean 'now we do not recall the past.'
segments.s198-g52: 'به بیداد شد کشته' is a passive construction: 'به بیداد' ('by/through injustice') marks the agent/instrument, 'شد' functions here as an auxiliary forming the passive with the past participle 'کشته' ('slain/killed'). The final phrase 'او‌گر بداد' is an if-clause ('اوگر' = 'if') with 'بداد' ('oppression')—i.e. 'if indeed it was oppression.' The line therefore reads that he was slain by injustice, with the conditional remark appended.
segments.s198-g53: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'خلعت ستد' is verb + object 'took the robe' (خلعت = robe of honour). The names 'گیو گودرز' appear as the agents (poetic order places the verb before the names). 'ز شاه' ('from the king') marks the source/possessor from whom the robe was taken. The construction is a compact, inverted poetic sequence: 'when [they] took the robe, Giv and Goudarz, from the king.'
segments.s198-g54: The conjunction 'که' here introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). 'آن کوه هیزم' reads appositively as 'that mountain (as) firewood' or 'that mountain's wood', and 'بسوزد' is the subjunctive/potential form 'may be burned' (prefix ب- with سوختن). 'براه' ('for/on the road') marks the intended use/place. Together: 'so that that mountain may be burned as firewood for the road.'
segments.s198-g55: 'کنونست' is an archaic fused form meaning 'it is now' (کنون + است). 'هنگام آن سوختن' is a temporal noun phrase: 'the time of that burning' (هنگام = 'time/occasion', آن سوختن = 'that burning' as genitive/complement). The clause states that now is the moment for that burning.
segments.s198-g56: 'به آتش سپهری برافروختن' is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase describing the action: 'to kindle (برافروختن) a fire (آتش) that is سپهری (here an epithet: 'celestial' / 'heavenly' in poetic usage)'. 'به' marks the target/goal of the action. The whole phrase functions as the announced action for that time: to set ablaze a celestial/heavenly fire (poetically phrased).
segments.s198-g81: ز + noun here marks cause/source in a poetic register: 'ز آتش' = 'from/because of the fire'. 'سه هفته' is a temporal phrase 'three weeks'. 'گذرشان' uses گذر in the sense 'passage/respite (of time)' with -شان as an enclitic referring to the people/situation; 'نبود' is simple past negative 'there was not'. The whole phrase means 'there was no passing/respite of three weeks (from the fire)'.
segments.s198-g82: This is a poetic enumeration introduced by repeated ز ('from/through'): each noun is given as another source of trouble. 'تف' here is an isolated noun (likely 'embers/sparks' or similar) but the provided sense list does not match, so it's marked unknown. 'زبانه' is the 'tongue/flare (of a flame)'. The sequence ends with 'باد' (wind) and 'دود' (smoke), joined by و 'and'.
segments.s198-g83: Here 'چهارم' is a temporal adverbial ('on the fourth'). Subject 'سپه' (army) is followed by the infinitive 'برگذشتن' ('to pass by / to move on') and the past verb 'گرفت' used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'began to' (lit. 'took to passing'). The clause thus reads 'on the fourth the army began to move on.'
segments.s198-g84: همان here means 'then/thereupon' or 'likewise'. 'آب و آتش' are the nouns 'water and fire'. 'نشستن گرفت' repeats the infinitive+گرفت pattern: 'نشستن' ('to sit; to settle') plus 'گرفت' = 'began to settle' or 'began to subside/come to rest'. The clause says that water and fire began to settle/abate.
segments.s198-g85: This clause is introduced by چو 'when'. 'سپهبد' (the spahbed) is the agent; the embedded clause 'لشکر برو گرد شد' describes the army turning about ('لشکر' = army; 'برو' + 'گرد شد' = 'turned about/was turned around'). Grammatically the commander is placed before the temporal clause, so the sense is 'when the spahbed caused/ordered the army to turn'.
segments.s198-g86: ز آتش gives the reason/cause ('because of/from the fire'). 'براه' is an adverbial phrase 'on the road / en route'. 'گروگرد شد' uses گروگرد (a throng/crowd) with شد 'became' to mean 'became thronged/crowded'. So: 'because of the fire the road became thronged.'
segments.s198-g87: 'سپاه اندر آمد' is a simple past clause 'the army came/entered' (اندر = 'in/into'). 'چنانچون سزد' is a parenthetical manner phrase: چنانچون = 'just as/like' and 'سزد' is an impersonal/poetic present meaning 'it befits / is proper'. Together: 'the army entered, as was fitting.'
segments.s198-g88: همه quantifies the following nouns 'کوه و هامون' ('every mountain and plain'). 'سراپرده زد' is a compound verbal phrase: سراپرده (inner-chamber/curtain, here used for tents or inner tents) plus زد (past 'struck/put up') = 'they set up tents (on)'. Word order is quantifier + places + verb, so 'they put up (their) tents on every mountain and plain.'
segments.s198-g57: گشاده (adjective) precedes the auxiliary شود (third-person singular subjunctive/optative here) to produce a passive/state sense: 'may/become open'. راه لشکر is a genitival phrase 'the road/way of the host' (راه + لشکر), with the noun phrase placed after the predicate as is common in epic Persian; مگر at the end introduces the conditional 'unless'.
segments.s198-g58: بباشد is the subjunctive/existential form 'may there be' or 'there be' used after مگر to state the exceptional case. سپه را uses را as a topic/definite marker pointing to 'the host' as the relevant party. بروبر modifies گذر: an adverbial sense 'directly/straight' (here 'straight/through'), and گذر is 'pass/passage' (a place to cross). The whole segment yields 'unless there be a direct pass for the host.'
segments.s198-g59: بدو is the prepositional compound 'to him/unto him' (introducing the indirect object). گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (past) though the provided senses list does not include the speech-verb sense, so it functions as the quotation introducer. گیو is the proper name subject of the reporting verb. این سخن is 'this speech/word/plan' and رنج نیست is a copular negation 'is not hardship/trouble' (نیست = 'is not'). Word order places the quoted clause after the reporting phrase.
segments.s198-g60: وگر هست = 'but if it is' (وگر = 'if / but if', هست = copula 'is'). هم here reinforces 'even so' / 'also'. رنج بی‌گنج نیست is a sentential negation saying the hardship is not 'without treasure' — i.e. not unrewarding: بی‌گنج='without treasure/unrewarding' and نیست negates that; the effect is proverbial: even if it is hardship, it is not fruitless.
segments.s198-g61: غمی گشت is a verbal phrase 'became sorrowful/distressed' (غمی = 'a sorrow/grief' functioning as the predicate that 'became' with گشت = 'became'). بیژن is the logical subject (Bijan). بدین داستان is an adverbial/causal phrase 'at/to this tale' (ب + دین/دین here together mean 'with/at this' followed by داستان 'tale'). Word order puts the state-phrase before the subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s198-g62: نباشم is a first-person negative subjunctive/volitional 'I will not / let me not be' (here reported speech: 'I will not be...'). بدین again 'in/with this' (introducing the object/affair). گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' placed after the quoted utterance (classical word order). همداستان is the predicate noun 'accomplice/like-minded one' (the role he refuses). Overall: he says he will not be an accomplice in this affair.
segments.s198-g63: مرا ... نباید نشست is an impersonal construction meaning 'one should not seat me' / 'I ought not to be seated'; مرا (here shown split as م + را) marks the person affected ('me'). با جوانی 'with the young' indicates the company in which seating would be improper. نباید is the impersonal 'should not / it must not be' and نشست is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to seat / to sit', so the literal structure is 'It should not be that one seat me with the young.'
segments.s198-g64: بپیری is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in/with old age' (the prefix ب- + پیری 'old age'). کمر 'waist/girdle' and بست (from بستن) 'he bound/tied' form the verb phrase 'he girded (his) loins' where بر میان تو ('upon/against your middle') gives the target/position. The line thus describes someone girding his belt (preparing himself) in old age against 'you' — a compact, idiomatic way to say he braced himself to oppose you even in old age.
segments.s198-g33: مبادا is a classical prohibitive formula meaning “may it not be / lest”; it introduces the wish/prohibition that follows. برین is composed of the preposition بر + the demonstrative-like ending ین (here functioning like ‘this/these’), so it means “on this/these”; بوم is the noun “land, native soil.” و simply links the next noun phrase. برها is a plural/collective notion of بر (here functioning as ‘heights/terraces/grounds’), and درود is a noun “greeting/benediction.” The clause lacks an explicit verb (a typical poetic ellipsis) so the construction reads as “May there not be greeting upon this land and upon those heights.”
segments.s198-g34: This segment gives specific place-names as further loci of the wish. کلات and سپدکوه‌گر are proper toponyms (treated as coordinated nouns). کاسه رود here functions as a compound/place reference (literally “bowl/ basin of the river” — Kaseh Rūd), and رود is the noun “river.” The verb is again omitted; the phrase is understood as continuing the prohibitive wish from the previous segment: “(nor) upon Kalat and the Kaseh-Rud of Sepdkuhgar.”
segments.s198-g35: ز is the poetic/prepositional marker “from, out of,” marking source. گردان is the noun “troop/unit,” modified by the adjective سرافراز “triumphant/lofty”; the proper name بهرام is the agent/subject. گفت is the past-tense verb “said/spoke.” Word order places the source phrase before the verb for emphasis: “From the triumphant troop Bahram said...”
segments.s198-g36: که introduces an indirect speech clause “that ….” این is the demonstrative “this” (the thing just mentioned). از + سپهبد marks the person affected/concerned (“from / to the spahbed” in a passive sense). نشاید is an impersonal/modal classical form meaning “it is not fitting / must not”; it governs the following past participle-like adjective نهفت “hidden/kept secret.” Together they form the impersonal: “it is not fitting that this be hidden from the spahbed.”
segments.s198-g37: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun “you.” ما را uses the object marker را to mark “us” as the direct object. بگفتار is a prepositional/adv phrase (بـ + گفتار) meaning “by/with words” or “according to speech,” and خامش here functions as a noun ‘silence’ (i.e. “to silence / bring silence”). کنی is the present-subjunctive/imperative 2nd-person form of کردن used to express obligation or command: “you should/must make (us) ….” The order is canonical for Persian: subject → object → adverbial → verb (with را marking the object).
segments.s198-g38: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle often meaning “indeed / will” (classical usage); رزم is the noun “battle” acting as the object of کنی. پور + سیاوش form a patronymic/compound meaning “son of Siavash” (پور is a name-element ‘son’). The final کنی is again the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of کردن: here it says the addressee is to perform the action: “you shall fight the battle for the son of Siavash.” Note that classical Persian freely allows noun phrases to appear before the verb, which completes the predication.
segments.s198-g39: مکن is the negative imperative “do not.” کژ and خیره are adjectives modifying ابر: کژ “crooked/wrong” and خیره “staring/astonished,” forming a vocative or metaphorical description (“O crooked, staring cloud”). بر is the preposition “on/at,” followed by کار راست “the right/just deed” (کار = deed, راست = right/true). The clause thus forbids the metaphorical ‘cloud’ from gloating or gazing perversely at a just action. Word order: negative imperative + modifiers + subject + prepositional object.
segments.s198-g40: بیک is a vocative/title (“bey/chieftain”); جان here is used in the sense “life” or “with your life” (an adverbial expression meaning ‘at the cost of your life’ or ‘with your life as stake’). نگه کن is a two-word imperative: نگه (gaze/watch) + کن (do) → “keep watch / look.” که introduces a causal/explanatory clause “for/because …”: چندین means “many/several,” and بکاست here is a classical/perhaps dialectal verb meaning “was diminished / fell off / became less” (in context, “many have been lost or diminished”). The whole reads as an urgent injunction: “O bey, keep watch with your life, for many have dwindled.”
segments.s198-g97: فرستاد is a past-tense verb meaning 'sent/dispatched' (here purposeful dispatch). گردی functions as a noun describing the group sent (here treated as a roaming/band—poetic use), and هم 'also' links this action to previous actions. اندر شتاب places the action 'in/with haste' (prepositional phrase after the verb). Word order is verb + object + adverbial phrase, typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s198-g98: بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional phrase meaning 'near/close at hand'; it marks the destination/position. چوپان is a noun 'shepherd' used as an epithet or identifier for افراسیاب; here the name follows the descriptor (typical appositive order). The whole segment specifies whom/where the detachment was sent.
segments.s198-g99: کبوده modifies نام and means 'darkened/bruise-like'—a poetic way to say reputation is stained. بدش is بد + ش ('bad' + 'his') forming 'his bad' (i.e., 'his bad reputation'). و شایسته بود adds 'and he was deserving/appropriate' with بود as the past copula; here شایسته means 'worthy' (of the bad reputation). The clause describes condition plus judgment.
segments.s198-g100: بشایستگی is a noun 'worthiness/appropriateness' and is the subject of the clause; نیز 'also' links this idea to the previous statement; بایسته بود uses بایسته (adjective 'fitting/proper') plus بود (past 'was') to assert that the fittingness itself was present. Together the clause emphasizes that the judgment was proper.
segments.s198-g101: بدو is a prepositional dative 'to him/unto him' marking the addressee. گفت is the narrative past 'said' (here given in the text as the speech verb). چون introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when/as'; تیره گردد سپهر is 'the sky becomes dark' with تیره 'dark' and گردد (subjunctive/present/future-form of 'become') used in subordinate temporal clause; سپهر is the noun 'sky'. The clause functions as the protasis of a conditional/temporal instruction.
segments.s198-g102: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. ز ایدر برو is an instruction: ز (from) + ایدر (here) + برو (imperative 'go/leave'), so 'leave from here'. هیچ منمای چهر is a negative prohibition: هیچ 'ever/at all' (here 'never/at all'), منمای 'do not show' (negative jussive/imperative form), and چهر 'face' — 'do not show your face at all.' The sequence gives a direct command following the temporal clause.
segments.s198-g103: نگه کن is an imperative compound: نگه (look/keep watch) + کن (imperative 'do'), meaning 'watch/keep watch'. که introduces an indirect question/subordinate clause 'that/which' — چندست 'how many are' asks the quantity. ز ایران سپاه specifies 'of/from Iran the army' (poetic preposition ز = 'from/of'). The clause asks the watcher to count or observe the Iranian forces.
segments.s198-g104: ز گردان 'from the battalions' specifies which contingents are meant (ز = 'from/of'). که دارد links a relative clause 'which have/possess' with دارد (present/past-habitual 'has'). درفش و کلاه are the items possessed — 'banner/standard and helmet' — describing the battalions' equipment. The whole phrase clarifies which troops to count.

sentences.s198-s1: After that, news reached Afrasiab that the frontier of Turan had become like a sea of water.
sentences.s198-s2: The army came into the Kaseh-rud area; the land was blackened because of Siavash's vengeance.
sentences.s198-s3: The spahbed said to Piran-salar that the king had disclosed words hidden before.
sentences.s198-s4: Unless we accept this speech of vengeance, let us all take up the standard and the tabireh (i.e. rally under banner and weapon).
sentences.s198-s65: Bahram said to him: “Tazhāv was with me, like a bull fighting a ferocious lion.”
sentences.s198-s66: They cut off his head with a dagger; the vile one then bound it to the Kayanian saddle.
sentences.s198-s67: He brought an army and scattered them in humiliation; there was neither renown nor a band of horsemen.
sentences.s198-s61: Who was the one who sent you here, and whom did you seek among these great men for advantage?
sentences.s198-s62: Bahram said, 'If you give an assurance, I will tell you whatever you ask about the matter.'
sentences.s198-s63: He is Tžāv (or Tžāvost), O king; I am his envoy — I am near him and am his devotee.
sentences.s198-s64: Do not kill me; let me show you the way to the place where he has his tomb.
sentences.s198-s37: I have no sorrow at this my going, for I could burn the flinty mountain with a single breath.
sentences.s198-s38: It passed with great difficulty through the basin-stream's gate; the whole world's distress was snow-melt.
sentences.s198-s39: When firewood came up upon that mountain's summit, he could not gauge its height or breadth.
sentences.s198-s40: From the fiery arrows a blaze was kindled; (they) were cast into the mountain, and the firewood burned.
sentences.s198-s13: Many people and quadrupeds were ruined; there was not one who had claw or arm in its place.
sentences.s198-s14: The high sun rose in my heaven; the world became all like a sea of water.
sentences.s198-s15: The spahbed caused the army to turn about, and talk of the day of battle continued for a while.
sentences.s198-s16: For here the army was ruined by shortage; if it is fitting, let us withdraw from this battlefield.
sentences.s198-s9: The inner rooms and the tents froze; a thread ran from the mountain over the snow.
sentences.s198-s10: For a week no one saw the face of the plain; the whole realm was made invisible by the snow.
sentences.s198-s11: Food, sleep, and resting-places grew scarce; you said that the face of the earth turned to stone.
sentences.s198-s12: No one remembered the day of battle; people were killing war-horses and eating them.
sentences.s198-s21: Still — what harm may yet befall you? At this time the buffalo is within its hide.
sentences.s198-s22: The spahbed thus said: 'Kâzar‑Gashasp was not more renowned in war than Zarsap.'
sentences.s198-s23: With an army keep watch, for when you encounter Riyuniz you will see his manhood and his appearance as well.
sentences.s198-s24: No — he did not fall slain upon the innocent; it was written thus: 'it was (what it was).'
sentences.s198-s5: Otherwise, if a host comes from Iran, we shall see neither the sun bright nor the moon.
sentences.s198-s6: Go — bring the army up from every side on high; words must not be allowed to grow long.
sentences.s198-s7: From that side a fierce gale arose, so that no one from Iran had any remembrance of battle.
sentences.s198-s8: A certain swift cloud came in, and when (it) whirled like dust from the cold it made the lips (meet) the teeth (with cold).
sentences.s198-s57: He bent the bow and pressed the flank; the heavy beast sprang from its place.
sentences.s198-s58: He fired a single arrow; whether (the dark-blue) lip was opened or not, nothing of the dark (thing) was visible in the night.
sentences.s198-s59: He struck at the shepherd's belt of the king; his color turned dark-blue, black.
sentences.s198-s60: He fell from his horse and cried for mercy; Bahram said to him, 'Tell the truth.'
sentences.s198-s33: We raised you on rice and with hardship; by words we never hurt you.
sentences.s198-s34: I must be taken into this business; it is not fitting that you should labor while I sit.
sentences.s198-s35: Giv said to him, ‘I who built it have taken up my neck for this task.’
sentences.s198-s36: Now, O son, is the time for adornment — not the time of old age and mercy.
sentences.s198-s45: As was fitting, they formed (their lines); from every side the vanguard dashed forth.
sentences.s198-s46: There sat a throng, a brilliant assembly — a body of riders whose brightness was with a lion's glow.
sentences.s198-s47: You had a respite at that place; you piled (mounds) so that mountain met mountain.
sentences.s198-s48: News came that an army had arrived from Iran; the flocks must be driven to one side of the road.
sentences.s198-s53: From here let us fall upon them in a night-raid; let us make every hill into the battlefield of Hamun.
sentences.s198-s54: A dark-blue mass came like a black cloud; the dark night drew near the Iranian host.
sentences.s198-s55: At the vanguard of the dark night was Bahram; his lasso was a snare for the elephant’s head.
sentences.s198-s56: The dark steed uttered a cry; from the host Bahram raised a shout.
sentences.s198-s25: To me a cup from him was filled with wine and milk; to the young man, from above, a word was like an arrow.
sentences.s198-s26: Now we no longer remember the past; he was slain by injustice — if indeed it was injustice.
sentences.s198-s27: When Giv and Goudarz took the robe from the king, so that that mountain might be burned as firewood for the road.
sentences.s198-s28: Now is the time of that burning, to kindle a celestial fire.
sentences.s198-s41: For three weeks there was no respite from the fire — from sparks, from the tongues of flame, from wind and from smoke.
sentences.s198-s42: On the fourth the army began to move on; likewise water and fire began to settle.
sentences.s198-s43: When the spahbed had the army turn about, the road became thronged because of the fire.
sentences.s198-s44: The army entered; as was fitting, they struck tents on every mountain and plain.
sentences.s198-s29: Let the host's way be opened, unless there is a direct pass for the army to go through.
sentences.s198-s30: Giv said to him, 'This plan (or talk) is no hardship; and if it is, hardship is not without reward.'
sentences.s198-s31: Bijan became distressed at this tale; 'I will not be an accomplice in this,' he said.
sentences.s198-s32: I ought not to be seated among the young; in old age he girded his loins against you.
sentences.s198-s17: May no greeting come upon this land and those heights — (nor upon) Kalat and Sepdkuhgar's basin (Kaseh Rud).
sentences.s198-s18: From the triumphant troop Bahram spoke, saying that this ought not to be hidden from the spahbed.
sentences.s198-s19: You should, by word, make us silent; you should fight the battle for the son of Siavash.
sentences.s198-s20: Do not, crooked cloud, stare at the right deed; O bey, keep watch with your life, for many have been diminished.
sentences.s198-s49: He, too, dispatched a detachment in haste to the vicinity of Afrasiab the shepherd.
sentences.s198-s50: His reputation was tarnished and bad, and he deserved it; worthiness, too, was fitting.
sentences.s198-s51: He said to him, 'When the sky grows dark, you leave from here; do not ever show your face.'
sentences.s198-s52: Keep watch to see how many troops from Iran there are, of the battalions that carry banner and helmet.
